content.full_text:
July 7, 1975


Robert A.D. Schwartz
Northern Chair
California Democratic Party
45 Second Street, Suite 401
San Francisco, Calif. 94105

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-142

Dear Mr. Schwartz:

Thank you for your letter of June 24, 1975,
requesting an opinion with respect to contributions
from labor unions.

Your request for a formal opinion is denied
because your question raises no substantial, novel
question of interpretation of the Political Reform
Act. The Act does not prohibit contributions to be
made by labor unions so long as the contribution is
not made or arranged for by a lobbyist and so long
as the lobbyist does not act as the agent or inter-
mediary in the making of the contribution.

Although for the reason stated no formal opinion
will be issued, I hope the foregoing is sufficiently
helpful. If you have any further questions, I urge
you to contact a member of the Commission staff.

Sincerely,


Daniel H. Lowenstein,
Chairman, for the
Commission

DHL:jp

sections.conclusion: The Act does not prohibit contributions to be made by labor unions so long as the contribution is not made or arranged for by a lobbyist and so long as the lobbyist does not act as the agent or intermediary in the making of the contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether labor unions may make contributions under the Political Reform Act, and whether lobbyists have restrictions on arranging or acting as intermediaries in such contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Labor unions may make contributions under the Political Reform Act as long as a lobbyist does not make, arrange for, or act as an agent/intermediary in the contribution. The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion because the question raises no substantial, novel issue of interpretation.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Declination letter with substantive guidance provided despite denial of formal opinion request. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers but clear question and answer present in body text. Document is dated 1975 and references Opinion No. 75-142.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit contributions to be made by labor unions so long as the contribution is not made or arranged for by a lobbyist and so long as the lobbyist does not act as the agent or intermediary in the making of the contribution.

Schwartz Northern Chair California Democratic Party 45 Second Street, Suite 401 San Francisco, Calif. 94105 Re: Request for Opinion No. 75-142 Dear Mr. Schwartz: Thank you for your letter of June 24, 1975, requesting an opinion with respect to contributions from labor unions. Your request for a formal opinion is denied because your question raises no substantial, novel question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. The Act does not prohibit contributions to be made by labor unions so long as the contribution is not made or arranged for by a lobbyist and so long as the lobbyist does not act as the agent or inter- mediary in the making of the contribution. embedding.summary: The FPPC denied a request for a formal opinion from the California Democratic Party regarding labor union contributions, finding the question raises no substantial novel interpretation issue. However, the FPPC provided guidance that labor unions may contribute as long as lobbyists do not make, arrange, or intermediary the contributions.
